justification: All pass criteria met: (1) 4 bounded contexts identified — Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification. (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified as integration points, including vehicle-class enforcement spanning Fleet and Delivery contexts, and recalculation triggers crossing Delivery→Route boundaries. (3) Vehicle class rule correctly marked invariant:true (BR-1), stop time correctly marked invariant:false (BR-8), notification window correctly marked invariant:false (BR-7). (4) 8 acceptance criteria provided, well above the 4 minimum. (5) All three route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated (new delivery, failed delivery, driver deviation) in BR-4/5/6.

observations.text: Exceptional bounded context identification — 4 clearly delineated contexts with explicit ownership boundaries for each domain concept

Strong invariant reasoning — BR-1 and BR-2 correctly identified as true invariants with physical/regulatory justification, while recalculation triggers (BR-4/5/6) thoughtfully classified as non-invariant optimization policies
8 high-quality acceptance criteria with concrete Given/When/Then scenarios covering vehicle class filtering, recalculation, deviation, notification boundary, and zone-specific stop times
10 well-identified edge cases covering concurrency, multi-driver zones, zone boundaries, shift lifecycle, and notification retraction — all with clear expected behaviors
Escalations are exceptionally well-structured with multiple options and recommendations for each ambiguity, directly useful for MJ and product stakeholders
AC-8 specifically tests cross-context vehicle class constraint in a multi-driver scenario, demonstrating understanding of the integration point

Recalculation triggers (BR-4/5/6) marked as invariant:false — the rubric expected invariant:true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's reasoning (they're optimization policies) is defensible but diverges from the expected classification
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the significant spec ambiguities identified, with clear separation of high and low confidence areas

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Fleet/Driver Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification); cross-context dependencies explicitly called out (vehicle class as Fleet→Route dependency, recalculation triggers as Delivery→Route events, notification as Route→Customer dependency); correct invariant classification (vehicle class BR-1 = true, stop time BR-8 = false, notification window BR-7 = false); 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceeding the 4 minimum; route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated (new delivery, failed delivery, manual deviation).

observations.text: Identified 4 bounded contexts clearly: Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, and Customer Notification
Vehicle class constraint (BR-1) correctly marked invariant: true with strong justification about physical and legal impossibility
Stop time (BR-8) and notification window (BR-7) correctly marked invariant: false
10 acceptance criteria with well-structured Given/When/Then format, covering happy paths and edge cases like duplicate notification prevention
Excellent edge case coverage including concurrent recalculation, multi-driver partitioning, notification oscillation, and invalid stop time values
5 well-structured escalations with options and recommendations covering multi-driver partitioning, deviation definition, and dispatcher authority
Cross-context dependencies surfaced as integration points — particularly the recalculation triggers spanning Delivery→Route contexts and vehicle class spanning Fleet→Route

Route recalculation triggers (BR-4, BR-5, BR-6) marked invariant: false — the rubric expected invariant: true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's justification is reasonable (batching or deferring could be valid policies) but diverges from rubric expectation
Zone constraint (BR-2) identified as an additional hard invariant beyond the rubric requirements
Rich ubiquitous language section with 11 well-defined terms supporting domain-driven design

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) 4 bounded contexts identified and named (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification). (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified — escalations call out multi-driver allocation across Fleet/Route/Delivery contexts, and the business_context notes the feature is 'an orchestration across all four contexts.' (3) Vehicle class constraint correctly marked invariant:true (BR-1), stop time correctly marked invariant:false (BR-7), notification window correctly marked invariant:false (BR-6). (4) 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria, well above the 4 minimum. (5) All three route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated (new delivery added, delivery marked failed, driver manual deviation) in BR-3/4/5 and the ubiquitous language.

observations.text: Exceptional bounded context identification — 4 clearly named contexts with ownership boundaries defined for each
Cross-context dependencies surfaced as escalations with concrete options and recommendations, directly actionable for MJ
Invariant classifications are correct across all 8 business rules, with strong justifications explaining why each is or isn't invariant
10 acceptance criteria covering happy path, vehicle class filtering, all 3 recalculation triggers, notification lifecycle, duplicate prevention, zone override, and empty state
Rich edge case analysis (10 scenarios) covering concurrency, GPS jitter, zone boundary changes, and vehicle breakdowns — goes well beyond minimum requirements
Route recalculation correctly identified as event-driven with BR-3/4/5 marking the three distinct triggers

BR-3/4/5 (route recalculation triggers) marked as invariant:false. A reasonable argument exists that these should be invariant:true since the system MUST always recalculate on these events per the spec. Bird's justification (stale routes don't corrupt data) is defensible but debatable — the rubric expected invariant:true for recalculation triggers
Confidence level appropriately low (52) given the significant spec gaps identified, with clear separation of high vs low confidence areas
AC-7 and AC-8 address notification deduplication and window-exit scenarios — edge cases that many analyses would miss
